## [00:00 - 03:04]

Now let's talk about dynamic product ads for your retargeting. So if you're running e-commerce and you've got more than one product or more than one product variant, you should be using dynamic product ads because it's a great way of retargeting people that have viewed a particular product with the same product in their newsfeed. Now in particular, I'm not going to go through the entire setup of how to do DPA. That information is below, but what I want to show you is how I would set up a DPA ad. Now the way I'm kind of looking at this is similar to when I'm setting up my Walmart's, I want to create something which is low maintenance. Again CBO helps me to do that in terms of budget allocation, but I also help Facebook with the variations that I put in and the way I structure my campaigns as well. So this particular example is a multi-product e-commerce store and it's a fairly established one as well. And I want to show you the setup that we've put in place here and how it operates. So we have a few different audiences here that we're letting the CBO budget push budget into these different areas based on performance. So you can see overall for this particular, this is 30 days results. So for this particular period, we spent 10K generated of 5 ROAS, which for the bottom of this particular funnel is good, it's okay. But we can break that down further and look at our specific audiences. So we've really kept it simple where we've tested different types of audiences and we've found that this works. So as you all know from previous training, when I set up my retargeting audiences, I like to go just generally for 30 days, add cart view content for example, for Recon and then start to refine it based on that. And in this particular one, we've split Add to Cart into people that have added to cart in the last 14 days and then people that have added to cart 14 to 28 days. Same for view content as well, because that's what seems to work best for this particular scenario. This e-com store has an average order value of anywhere between $80 and $100. People don't always buy on this first visit and therefore a lot of the sales do then fall into retargeting. But what we've also found is there's a good delayed attribution where people will still continue buying 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 days after the first clicked, all the way up to 14 and then it starts to die down. And that's why we've put that audience here, because 14 days is the time span we've identified through attribution as to when those sales start to die out for retargeting. And you can kind of see that here in the performance as well. We've got 10 ROAS here from 0 to 14 days from the point that I've added to cart and

## [03:04 - 06:07]

then the ROAS is smaller here. And Facebook have also then managed the budget in that way as well. So most of the budget for the Etto cart has gone into this campaign and a smaller budget here. Now, when you look at ad set budgets and how you'd set them previously and how we used to always do it as well, is we would have to manually set these budgets and then monitor them every day based on performance, increase them, decrease them, etc. But now with CBO, Facebook does that for us and it's always balancing that budget out. So that takes away the budget management and also by us defining the date period from first visit to the site to retargeting, we're controlling where that spend goes because that's the best performance. So we know that this audience is our best. Facebook also then sees the data on conversion and ROAS. But it's also looking at other things such as how many impressions there are, what kind of click through rates coming through, what kind of frequency you're hitting to determine where the spend goes. So now if I change this view and show you the same two ad sets, but now show you the reach and the frequency, you can see here that our 14 day audience have a frequency of 20. So that's pretty high. But this is over 30 days. So is a frequency of 20 times in the last 30 days high? Not really. This is doing okay. But Facebook is still putting more spend here because the cost per purchase is better. So here we are optimizing for purchase. We've set the campaign up as the catalog format type. So you can quickly scan into setup to see what format is set up. So this campaign itself is $340 a day. It's set up as a catalog sales, no manual bid, no nothing. And this type of campaign is pretty much set and forget. So we've set the budget. We've given Facebook the ad sets to play within. And then Facebook optimizes within those ad sets as to which ads get served within our dynamic product catalog. Now if I kind of expand down, you can see that there are currently three ads running in this particular ad set. One of the things I really love about the ad formats is in particular the collection ad format. So with the dynamic product ad for retargeting, you can create a standard carousel which shows the image scrolling across the news feed. Or you can create a collection ad which only currently runs, we do it as an instant collection, runs in the mobile format, but it gives a better view of the actual creative and gives, I think the product a bit more space to operate.

## [06:07 - 09:09]

So let me show this one here. So just to show you an example, what we do is what I love about the collection format is showing a video that relates back to your product, the product experience, thinking about your 5W avatars and the barriers and reasons to buy. And then you use the carousel format below that to allow people to see what they've just recently viewed, for example. And these formats work extremely well. When Facebook is working, of course, then you can also see that as well. But the cool thing about this is when you are retargeting people with something that has a bit of emotion and not just something which just kind of shows the product, you're selling the opportunity. So what we're trying to do with this is to show people a new way, a new opportunity to consume the product, not just the product on its own. So I've seen people run dynamic product ads where they're just showing the product because that's what's coming through their product feed. So the way you set up your dynamic product ad is you create a feed. There are many ways to take your feed from your sales system. So the sales system could be through Shopify or WooCommerce or any other e-commerce platform. That data is fed into Facebook. Facebook then take, for example, your product descriptions, your product images, etc. Now what you show in your product image for your landing page could be quite different to what you really want to show in your ads. What this format allows you to do is run a static or video format in your main kind of real estate for the ad. You can put some kind of, you know, we put customer testimonial copy here, but it also allows us to have a carousel further down as well. So by this point, someone's been through our funnel, they've landed on the site. In this particular case, they've also added to cart. So there's high intent there. We don't really need to go and explain who the brand and product is, what it's about, etc. Because we'd assume that they've been through that already. This video format is a real simple one created in Facebook through their own video editor tool by just dropping some images in. There's no reason you can't do the same. And then what we're doing is pulling the feed through of the products that they've viewed and retarget them with that as well. And this format just works so well. And you can see here, for example, the carousel format has been performing okay, but we've introduced the collection format and it's performing that much more better as well. Now, you know, one thing I would say is when we are seeing performance like this, what we'll often do is we'll give it a chance. So we'll introduce a set of challenger ads, for example. So the ad below we stopped running it because of stock and kind of the images were shown. We've introduced these two ads here and they are crushing it in terms of performance so

## [09:09 - 12:12]

far. What I would do is pause this one out. So it gives these other ads an opportunity to run and start building up more data because they are performing so well. What CBO will do is it may end up biasing too much spend here. I would track this day by day to see if CBO is making the right decision to now start serving more traffic to these two ads. And if it doesn't pick up, then I would pause this one which has got the most spend and give these two ads a chance to pick up more data. And then I might introduce it again a week later because I know it's a good ad. Like there's no reason not to run it except for giving these two ads more space to perform as well. So that's the kind of structure I'm trying to set up because also by doing it this way and the whole kind of premise behind the set and forget is when someone's added to cart that 14-day audience is refreshing every 14 days. So if you add to cart today and if you see the ad over the next 14 days and you don't buy, you're exiting that audience and now you're falling into this one. New people are falling into this other ad set every single day. So the ads there generally don't fatigue because Facebook's managing the budget across the different ad sets. I'm managing the budget at the campaign level so I'm not over committing and therefore the performance here stabilizes because every day that audience is updating with new people, new people are seeing that ad, they're reacting to it, old people fall off the back end of that audience. So this ad set is constantly refreshing. So to kind of put that into context, if you think about a lookalike audience or a custom audience and let's say you create a lookalike of 1% of your purchases and you're targeting them at the top of your funnel, at some point your ad could end up reaching everyone depending on how long you're advertising and what your spend is and then you'll start to saturate that audience because they've now seen your ad so many times. So let's say for example you take a 1% audience in the US, you're targeting 1% of purchases, you're spending let's say 100k a day on ads, you're going to reach those purchases quite quickly and you're going to start racking up those frequencies quite quickly. That audience is static so once you create that 1% unless you're manually updating it or using dynamic website custom audiences, that audience can stay dynamic. This audience, so static, this audience is dynamic. This audience updates every day and every 14 days it's refreshing with new people. So I don't really have to worry about the audience. All I'm monitoring is at the end level to make sure things are performing. For example, making sure that we're not selling items or at least showing items that are not in stock or no longer sold and that's pretty much it.

## [12:12 - 15:13]

And the other thing is also making sure that you're managing your budget effectively as well. If I look for example at the top level and I want to look at whether I've got scope to push more spend, then in this case absolutely. So over the last 30 days my frequency is 10 so if I break that down by week for example, I can see what the frequencies are across my ad sets and I look at this and say right on a given week we're hitting people five times over a seven day period. That's pretty low. So there's opportunity here to actually up my budget and as you know from how I've been kind of describing how to deal with frequency especially further down the funnel, frequency is your friend. When it comes to retargeting you're allowed to hit your customers more frequently because there's a difference between someone who's never seen your brand or your product before and what their expectation is. So if you spam your user and your prospect in the news feed, they don't know anything about you and they haven't engaged in any ads, they haven't clicked and they keep seeing your ad time and time and time again. It's not necessarily going to prompt them to go and click on your ad. However, if they've visited your site and they've viewed a product and they've gone to the effort of adding to cart, that's a pretty hot prospect. So hitting them if they see an ad even twice a day, once or twice a day that's kind of okay. So if I had a frequency of 7 to 10 here a week, I'd say that's about right. So that's how I gauge my budgets. So now what I would be doing is on my action here is to increase the budget to push the frequency up and to get the balance between the frequency I'm seeing and the performance I'm seeing. So right now for example, I'm averaging a return on investment of five, I'm edging the budget up by 20 to 40% a day and tracking the ROAS and CPA as I go. So I would be up in the budget waiting at least 24 hours, maybe even 48 hours to see how that additional budget settles across my different ad sets and then give it a chance to steady before I look to bump budgets again. So ultimately your goal is to spend as much as you can in retargeting. That's it. However, you don't want to burn out your audiences by upping your budget too much. So if I'm spending 340 a day right now, I'd love to spend 3,000 a day. But if I do that, my frequency is going to go up, people are going to get annoyed. They will send that negative feedback through the ad to my account. My performance will drop, my CPMs will go up and then I'll be in a world of pain. So like fun for now, for example, if I look at my CPMs and that should be here, my CPMs right now are pretty good. Like I'm getting great results on my CPMs because the quality of my audience and the

## [15:13 - 18:19]

stability of what I'm doing is pretty good. So for a retargeting audience, this is good, $7 overall and you can see the split on the different audiences of my CPM. And it's kind of, it's in a good place. So as I'm increasing my budgets further down the funnel, I keep an eye on frequency. I'm making sure my CPMs stay on par to kind of where they are. I'm also tracking click-through rate and making sure click-through rate and cost per outbound click don't fluctuate too much as well. And ultimately making sure my metrics remain stable. So if I'm getting an overall $10 CPA for my retargeting and I'm upping my budget and I'm maintaining that $10 and all maintaining that five ROAS, then I know I'm in a good space. So that's kind of how I look at Ecom catalog ads. I want to set them up. I want to do some iterative tests early on. So this is an established account. So we know what kind of images and copy to run. Early on we're doing that testing. We're testing different angles and reasons to buy and different functional reasons. We're doing all that testing in our hot campaign. So I've talked previously about that. We do our testing in the hot campaign because we don't have enough traffic to do the full grad testing. So here, for example, we introduced these collection ads. Didn't know whether they're going to perform, but we're doing our testing here because we have a good idea of who our customer is. It's an established account. We have prior data for the carousel that worked and the type of messaging. And all we're doing is changing up the videos and images and testing to see if we can get a better performance, which as you can see we are. So that's the kind of overall structure. It's really simple. I know some people like to really overcomplicate it and have add to cart zero to three days, three to five, five to 10, 10 to 14, test it. I prefer broad because the bigger the audience is, the better the stability. But if there's a reason to split it, then split it. For us, the 14 and 14 to 28 days works best. Same for view content. And also this campaign here is an audience which some people often miss out. So if these are people that have added to cart in the last 28 days, these are people that have viewed a product over the last 28 days, what about people that have done neither? So they could be blog visitors. They could be organic and they've just come through the homepage. There could be many ways in. And we're running that and you can see here there's a potential. And actually we're doing that in this campaign here, in this account. So this is just one of the retargeting campaigns. We have other retargeting campaigns running which allow us to test different pockets of audience, different ad types, et cetera. Because it's not always the case that we're going to get performance from all of, just

## [18:19 - 21:19]

from DPA. So for example, if we're running catalog sales here, we're running other ads in alongside it as well because we want to test non-DPA format as well, DCO for different purposes, et cetera, et cetera, because we want to see whether there is another opportunity for us to scale up. This is interestingly, the hot campaign is actually called in hot. So just when you are doing your naming convention, something often we do is we have the layer in there as a number as well. So if you are doing your naming convention, think about if you are, for example, in the case like this, you've got a campaign that contains photo, which HOT is part of. That's why we always use a number as well. You may notice we have one for cold, two for warm, et cetera. We just have that kind of method to make sure we have the correct funnel as well. So as you can see, HOT here, we have catalog sales, we have a DCO, which is paused due to this campaign promotional which is running. And then we've got standard website conversion ads, video and static. And we're running them together. Now, the audiences are similar. There's two ways of doing this. One, you can run them in the same campaign, so you can duplicate ads, ad sets. But for example, because we're running catalog sales alongside the others, you're running DPA ads, dynamic product ads within that as well. So here we've got our catalog sales objective collected. We've got our budget set. And then just to show you the setup, we then do our audience selection within. But one of the important things is we select our catalog. One thing that I do also like to test and play around with is taking our catalog and creating subsets of it as well. And what I mean by that is, for example, we're at the moment with this particular campaign, we've created a subset of the catalog, but only apparel. So the store sells other products, but we're only retargeting on apparel because it's a strategy that we want to focus on. It's also higher ticket items. Often we will create product sets where, for example, we, you know, our jewelry store, for example, we'll have jewelry items where we have kind of cross sales and bits and pieces for $5, $10, $15. I don't want to retarget you with that. I want to retarget you with items above $30, $40, so that when you see that in your newsfeed, there the purchases I want from you, because that's where my return on ad spend is going to come from. If I retarget you with a $5 accessory from my catalog and you end up buying it, it's not going to be of great benefit to me in terms of my return on ad spend. They are in my catalog because they're on my store, but they're only there to bump up the order value in my cart.

## [21:19 - 23:42]

So for example, if you add a necklace to your cart, we might give you, you know, a low-cost bracelet to go with it, but I don't want that bracelet necessarily appearing in my dynamic product ads. So within your DPA settings, you can create subsets of your, your, your catalog and you can bring them here. So your product set is based on that. So this one we've got only apparel. Then you set your targeting, which we've done here. So we're pulling people in based on the last 14 days. Here's the audience interaction. So people have added to cart in the last 14 days, but not purchased in the last 180. And we just go simply for automatic placements. And let's just Facebook decide where that's coming from. There's lots of different combinations you can create. So for example, post purchase, what I often like to do is create a catalog for upsell and cross-sell products. So you can then retarget people post purchase with a specific part of your catalog. So for example, giving you the simple examples, if for example you buy, let's say, some genes, you could cross-sell them with shirts. So let's say it's a men's apparel store, you might have a target based on people that bought genes and you cross-sell them with products that are complementary to that. How do you do that? There's multiple ways. One of the easiest ways is to create a custom audience based on your sales data of anyone that's bought genes. Let's just say that's your best seller and then retarget them with cross-sells and say, hey, these are great complementary products to go with your genes, for example. So there's loads of ways you can cross-sell and upsell in terms of your merchandising ideas. And I think Facebook really do a good job of enabling that, but I don't think a lot of people really understand how those opportunities can be placed. So in this account, we've kept it simple. You can go complex. It really depends on what niche you're in and what opportunities there are to cross-sell and upsell. But as I say, start off simple, develop it as you go, but create a system that is hands off and allows you to almost set and forget and just manage it at the highest level.
